2025 · Nature · Post-training & RL

DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

Pure reinforcement learning — no supervised fine-tuning, no human reasoning traces — incentivizes a base model to teach itself to reason. Verification, reflection, and an "aha moment" emerge on their own; the result matches OpenAI's o1 on math and code, and distills into models 400× smaller.

DeepSeek-AI — Daya Guo, Dejian Yang, Haowei Zhang, Junxiao Song, Peiyi Wang, …, Zhihong Shao, Zhuoshu Li, Ziyi Gao
venue Nature citations 5,472 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2024 the recipe for a reasoning model looked fixed: take a base LLM, supervise it on human-written chains of thought, then polish with RL. Chain-of-thought prompting and instruction-tuning on multi-step demonstrations had pushed math and code accuracy up — but every gain rode on annotated reasoning traces. That dependence has two costs the paper names directly: it does not scale (expert step-by-step solutions are expensive), and it caps the ceiling at human performance — a model trained to imitate human thought can never explore a better, non-human-like way to reason.

OpenAI's o1 had just shown that scaling the length of the reasoning trace at inference time works spectacularly, but the method was closed. The open question was sharp and load-bearing: can a model develop reasoning on its own, from nothing but a reward for getting the final answer right — skipping the supervised-fine-tuning (SFT) stage entirely? If "think step by step" is a skill an LLM can be incentivized to discover rather than taught to copy, the annotation bottleneck disappears and the human ceiling with it.

DeepSeek-R1 answers yes, and does it in the open: weights, distilled small models, and the full training recipe released. The result became the reference point for the entire 2025 wave of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) — and the first reasoning model published, peer-reviewed, in Nature.

// how it works

The method

Start with DeepSeek-V3-Base — a 671B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model (37B active per token) pretrained on 14.8T tokens. Apply RL directly, with no SFT first. The reward is not a learned neural model but a rule: an accuracy reward that checks the final answer against ground truth (a boxed number verified by a parser for math, a compiler running test cases for code), plus a format reward that the model wraps its thinking in <think>…</think> tags. Nothing constrains how the model reasons inside those tags.

Rewardrule  =  Rewardacc  +  Rewardformat // no neural reward model on reasoning tasks — the authors found those get reward-hacked at scale

The optimizer is Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), inherited from DeepSeekMath. PPO needs a separate value model, as large as the policy, to estimate how good each token is — doubling the memory of training. GRPO deletes it. For each question it samples a group of G outputs from the current policy, scores them all, and uses the group's own mean and standard deviation as the baseline: an answer is "good" exactly to the degree it beats its siblings on the same question.

Ai  =  ri − mean(r1, …, rG)std(r1, …, rG) // the advantage for output i — a group-normalized reward, no value network needed

Run this loop — 16 samples per question, batch of 512, ~10,400 update steps — and the model becomes DeepSeek-R1-Zero: a reasoner built with zero human reasoning data. But R1-Zero has rough edges. Its chains of thought are hard to read and it mixes English and Chinese mid-sentence, and because rule-based RL only covers verifiable domains it is weak at writing and open-domain Q&A.

So the released DeepSeek-R1 is not R1-Zero — it is a four-stage pipeline that buys back readability and generality without losing the reasoning:

  1. Cold start. Fine-tune V3-Base on a few thousand curated long-CoT examples with a clean, human-readable thinking style.
  2. Reasoning RL. The same rule-based RL as R1-Zero, plus a language-consistency reward (the fraction of the CoT in the target language) — which slightly lowers accuracy but kills the language mixing.
  3. Rejection-sampling SFT. Generate, filter for correctness, and SFT on a large mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data (writing, factual Q&A) — restoring general ability.
  4. Preference RL. A final RL stage adding a 66K-pair helpfulness reward model and a 106K-prompt safety reward model — applied only in the last 400 steps, because longer use of a model-graded reward starts to get hacked.
// what they found

The results

The headline is in the training curve. On AIME 2024, R1-Zero's pass@1 climbs from 15.6% to 77.9% over the RL run — and with self-consistency over 16 samples (cons@16) it reaches 86.7%, well past the average human competitor. No human solution was ever shown to it. Just as striking: with no length incentive in the reward, the model's average response grows from a few hundred to over 10,000 tokens. It learns, unprompted, that thinking longer pays.

What's growing inside those longer responses is the interesting part. The authors track the frequency of reflection words and of the single word "wait", and both jump sharply mid-training — the quantitative fingerprint of the now-famous "aha moment," where the model spontaneously stops, writes "Wait, wait. That's an aha moment," and re-derives a step. Self-verification, backtracking, and exploring alternatives were never in the reward; they are emergent.

The polished DeepSeek-R1 then reaches the frontier. On math it is on par with OpenAI-o1-1217 (AIME 2024 79.8 vs 79.2; MATH-500 97.3 vs 96.4), it leads on LiveCodeBench (65.9 vs 63.4) and sits at the 96.3rd Codeforces percentile, and it tops every model on MMLU-Pro (84.0). o1 keeps the edge on a few axes — GPQA Diamond (75.7 vs 71.5), engineering-style coding on Aider (61.7 vs 53.3), and short-form factuality on SimpleQA (47.0 vs 30.1). The multi-stage pipeline is what makes R1 a usable assistant: instruction-following (IF-Eval) jumps from R1-Zero's 46.6 to 83.3, and AlpacaEval 2.0 from 24.7 to 87.6.

BenchmarkDeepSeek-V3OpenAI o1-1217DeepSeek-R1
AIME 2024 (pass@1)39.279.279.8
MATH-500 (pass@1)90.296.497.3
GPQA Diamond (pass@1)59.175.771.5
LiveCodeBench (pass@1)36.263.465.9
Codeforces (rating)113420612029
MMLU (EM)88.591.890.8

Two more findings travel widely. First, distillation works and is cheaper than RL. SFT-ing open Qwen and Llama models on 800K samples generated by R1 (no RL on the student) produces tiny reasoners that beat far larger non-reasoning models: R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B scores 28.9 on AIME — above GPT-4o (9.3) and Claude-3.5-Sonnet (16.0) — and R1-Distill-Qwen-32B reaches 72.6 AIME / 94.3 MATH. Running large-scale RL directly on a 32B base (Qwen2.5-32B-Zero) only matches QwQ-32B-Preview (~47 AIME), far below the 72.6 of the distilled model. The lesson: distill a strong reasoner into a small one rather than RL the small one from scratch.

Second, R1 allocates test-time compute adaptively — fewer than 7,000 thinking tokens on easy problems, over 18,000 on the hardest — without any token budget being hand-set. And because its samples build on each other through backtracking, it dwarfs independent-sampling tricks: on AIME, majority voting lifts GPT-4o only from 9.3% to 13.4%, while R1 sits at 79.8% (and 86.7% with its own majority vote).

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The science is strong and the release is unusually complete, but the marketing line — "pure RL, no human labels, reasoning emerges" — describes R1-Zero, not the model you download. The released DeepSeek-R1 uses thousands of cold-start SFT examples, a large rejection-sampling SFT mix, 66K human-preference pairs, and 106K safety annotations. The clean "no supervision" story is real as a scientific demonstration of R1-Zero; it is not how the frontier assistant was actually built. Read the two claims separately.

The benchmarks themselves invite caution. AIME 2024 is a fixed, public, 30-problem competition set, and it is both the headline metric and a primary RL target over 10,400 steps — exactly the setup where a model can quietly overfit the evaluation. To their credit the authors probe this: a held-out RL run on a base frozen before o1 existed (Qwen2-Math-7B-Zero) still gains, and AIME 2025 holds up near AIME 2024 (18.1 vs 22.3). But the base model is the deeper worry — V3-Base was pretrained on web crawl the authors acknowledge "contain[s] a significant number of OpenAI-model-generated answers," so some reasoning ability may be indirect distillation from existing models rather than pure self-emergence.

And the method has a hard boundary the paper is candid about: it only works where a reliable verifier exists. The whole reason a neural reward model is banned from the reasoning stage — and capped to 400 steps elsewhere — is that it gets reward-hacked: optimize a learned reward too long and the proxy keeps rising while real quality falls (Fig. 7 below). So "just give the model the right incentive" silently excludes writing, open-ended judgment, and anything you can't grade with a rule — the open problem the rubric-reward and reward-hacking literature (OnlineRubrics, POW3R, Rubicon) picked up from here.

Finally, "emergent reasoning" rests partly on proxies. Counting "wait" frequency is evocative but does not establish that the trace causes the answer — the chain-of-thought-faithfulness question hangs over every reasoning model. And R1's own data shows RL is sharpening, not expanding: AIME pass@64 (90.0%) sits well above pass@1 (79.8%), so the capability ceiling was largely present in the base — consistent with DeepSeekMath's "RL lifts maj@K but not pass@K" finding.

baselines

Strong and contemporary — o1-1217, o1-mini, Claude-3.5-Sonnet-1022, GPT-4o-0513, DeepSeek-V3. Caveat: o1-1217 numbers are partly reported from public sources ("API access challenging in mainland China"), and the "1.5B beats the best closed models" line is measured against non-reasoning models, not o1-mini.

contamination

AIME 2024 is public and also an RL target; partially mitigated by a pre-o1 frozen base and an AIME 2025 check. The bigger risk is V3-Base's pretraining crawl containing OpenAI-generated answers — reasoning may be partly indirect distillation.

what's missed

The "no human labels" claim holds only for R1-Zero; released R1 uses SFT + 66K preference + 106K safety labels. Method is confined to verifiable domains. Emergence is shown via proxy metrics, and RL sharpens rather than raises the ceiling (pass@64 ≫ pass@1).

would raise confidence

CoT-faithfulness tests (is the trace causal?); more fresh held-out sets beyond AIME; full release of the reward-model and cold-start data (still partial); clean ablation isolating cold-start SFT vs RL contribution; reproducible o1 numbers.

// bottom line

A landmark, and a fair one: it genuinely demonstrates that reasoning can be incentivized rather than imitated, ships the weights and a distillation recipe to prove it, and is honest about its own limits (reward hacking, verifier dependence, sharpening-not-expanding). Just don't conflate the pure-RL demonstration (R1-Zero) with the multi-stage, SFT-and-reward-model product (R1) — the paper doesn't, even if the one-liner does.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Reasoning can be incentivized, not just imitated. Pure RL on a base model — rewarding only the correct final answer — drives R1-Zero's AIME 2024 from 15.6% to 77.9%, with self-verification and longer thinking emerging on their own. No human reasoning traces required.
  2. A rule beats a reward model where you can write one. Rule-based accuracy + format rewards sidestep the reward hacking that sinks neural reward models at scale (Fig. 7). The flip side: the recipe only reaches as far as your verifier does — which is why everything non-verifiable became the next research frontier.
  3. The released R1 is a pipeline, not pure RL. Cold-start SFT, rejection-sampling SFT, and preference/safety reward models buy back readability and generality — lifting instruction-following from 46.6 to 83.3 — and land R1 on par with OpenAI o1 on math and code.
  4. Distill, don't RL-from-scratch, for small models. SFT-ing on 800K R1-generated samples makes a 1.5B model out-reason GPT-4o on AIME; running large-scale RL directly on a 32B base can't catch the distilled 32B. Strong reasoning is cheap to transfer, expensive to grow.
